evidence: QueryExecutor interface documents every method with precise @param/@return/@throws Javadoc, consistently distinguishing executeBcqlQuery (BCQL parser, logDice-ranked) from executeCqlQuery (CQL parser, raw KWIC) — named similarly but with clearly separated contracts

@Nullable/@NonNull annotations from jspecify are applied consistently on return types and parameters across CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, and QueryExecutor, giving callers reliable nullability information

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() Javadoc (line 241) says 'Logs a warning when the field is present but unrecognised' but the implementation throws IllegalArgumentException — the documented and actual error contract diverge

WordSketchApiServer.start() converts IOException to undocumented IllegalStateException (line 123) with no @throws clause in the method Javadoc; callers have no textual signal that start() can throw a RuntimeException

CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency() correctly documents its dual contract: returns 0L when lemma annotation is missing (silent absence), and throws IOException for unexpected runtime failures — both paths are honoured in the implementation
wrapWithErrorHandling contract is explicit and precise: IAE→400, IOException→500, all other exceptions→500; this contract is consistent across all 15 registered routes
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution declares IllegalArgumentException for unrecognised CQL formats and enforces the [constraint]-prefix precondition in both Javadoc and implementation

exploreByPattern public overload validates relationType.isEmpty() and unsupported PosGroup before delegating to the package-private overload; the two-tier validation design is documented as intentional

strengths: QueryExecutor interface provides one of the clearest method-level contracts in the codebase: every method documents its null-tolerance, exception conditions, and distinctions from similar-named siblings
@Nullable/@NonNull annotations are used consistently on public and package-private APIs, giving callers reliable nullability contracts without relying on prose alone
wrapWithErrorHandling establishes a consistent, predictable exception-to-HTTP-status contract that is honoured by all 15 registered routes without exception
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() explicitly documents a 'two-tier exception contract' explaining why IOException is wrapped in IllegalStateException — callers know exactly what to expect
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution enforces a precondition (pattern must start with '[') in both Javadoc and code, and the precondition matches the real invariant imposed by all grammar configs
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are documentation-layer divergences rather than runtime behavioral violations — the code behaves correctly but two Javadoc comments describe actions (logging a warning, no thrown exception) that differ from what the implementation actually does.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase sits clearly above the 'significant drag' threshold (60): null contracts are annotated, exception propagation is consistent, no getter-named methods mutate state, and the QueryExecutor interface is an exemplary contract document. Two localized Javadoc inaccuracies — parseRelationType saying 'logs a warning' when it throws, and start() silently converting IOException to undocumented IllegalStateException — prevent a higher score but are genuinely minor in scope. The overall contract coherence is solid and developer-friendly, landing around 78: strong foundations with a handful of documentation inaccuracies rather than structural contract violations.

issues.summary: parseRelationType Javadoc says 'logs a warning' for unrecognised values but the code throws IllegalArgumentException
evidence: Javadoc (line 241): 'Logs a warning when the field is present but unrecognised, which typically indicates a typo in the grammar config.'
Implementation (lines 246-249): catch block re-throws IllegalArgumentException with a detailed message — no logging, no warning, immediate exception
issues.suggestion: Update the Javadoc to say '@throws IllegalArgumentException if the value is present but not a recognised RelationType name' and remove the 'Logs a warning' sentence, so the documented error contract matches the actual behaviour.

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer.start() converts IOException to IllegalStateException without any @throws Javadoc, leaving callers unaware of the unchecked exception
evidence: start() method (lines 118-130) has no Javadoc at all — no @throws, no contract description
Line 122-123: catches IOException from HttpServer.create() and rethrows as IllegalStateException('Failed to bind port ' + port, e)
The constructor Javadoc (lines 62-65) explains why binding is deferred to start(), but the start() method itself documents nothing about exceptions that can escape it
issues.suggestion: Add a Javadoc block to start() with '@throws IllegalStateException if the server cannot bind to the configured port, wrapping the underlying IOException' so callers at the call site in Main.java know to expect and handle (or log) this unchecked exception.

root_causes: Both issues appear to be Javadoc written before or after the implementation changed (parseRelationType likely started as a log-and-return-empty path and was later hardened to throw; start() may never have had Javadoc drafted)
The private scope of parseRelationType reduced the pressure to keep its Javadoc accurate since callers are all internal
